lines.text: "We say it to the kids and the crew, and we will say it to the mirror until the glass cracks. But there is no escaping gravity." That's how this chapter opens. It might be the most brutal thesis statement I've read in a while.
Right out the gate, Nyland is telling us this isn't a story about hope conquering all. This is a story about people who know they're falling and choose to fall with dignity.

And then we're thrown straight into chaos. The Dáinn is essentially having seizures—gravity surging, people vomiting, this desperate one-sided communication with the Brisinger. Morse code for titans. CQ CQ CQ. Naval code. We seek you.
The sensory details are relentless. Acid burning at the back of your throat. The bridge reeking of sweat and vomit. You're enduring it alongside them.

What fascinates me is how Nyland uses the chapter title—"Two Courses"—and then gives us these parallel narratives. You've got the crisis on the Dáinn, plotting two courses. One back to Mairee and the other, this Stingray rescue mission with Alistair Hale and Stacey Kiran, and then this completely different register with the Galley Draw ceremony where they are cooking a full-course meal together.
I loved the Galley Draw, Vera. Here's this ship in crisis, rationing food, facing something catastrophic, and they're still doing this ritual where rank gets stripped away.
"Just the meat that eats." What a line. And. The captain "coincidentally" drew Ionie's name? I don't think so. He wanted her at that table.
Oh, absolutely.

The ritual itself is beautifully constructed. The thumping, the shared bread, the wine. It's explicitly religious imagery, but grounded in something practical—fighting loneliness, building trust. An engine starter.
These aren't hardened space marines. They're people who are terrified and trying to hold onto something.
Such a contrast to Ulre Corbin and Eva Bellamy. That scene is doing something very different.
It's uncomfortable. Deliberately so.
He smells her wine for alcohol, disgusted because it reminds him of his mother...
Yeah. That tells me Corbin is not nostalgic about *his* home.
But Ulre immediately becomes physically aggressive. Or obsessive. Freud would have had a field day with Ulre.

The man practically walks around with unresolved psychodynamics stitched into his uniform. But I bet there's something wounded in *both* of them. They're both using each other, but I think there's genuine history there.
History, sure. But healthy? Absolutely not. I do love that even the bra is science fiction.
Ulre reading her heartbeat through the fibers is actually very on-brand for him though. The man needs measurable data before he believes anyone feels anything.,

Emotional vulnerability? Unacceptable. Real-time telemetry? Now we’re talking. And notice how it's intercut with Alistair and Stacey, who have this completely different dynamic—teasing. That moment where Stacey slams into Alistair's chest. And that leather-bound book of poetry.
And is Nyland teasing us with some foreshadowing?
Wings being unmade?
"Birds bore the earth, bound fast to soil and bone…" Pretty depressing.
Mythical. It’s about ascension. Transcendence. "And in the act of letting go, I remembered."
Yeah. Like the ego has to break apart before the person underneath can actually perceive reality again. Very gnostic.

But on Mairee? Congratulations, your ego has been liquefied by the fungal planet. Speaking of which—that image of a trickster leaning out of a "collapsing fractal doorway, wagging his finger." What is that? Is Alistair hallucinating during the Fold transit? Is it a memory? There's something psychedelic happening that we're not privy to yet.
Lava lamp ride.

Yeah. Alistair downplays it. There's more to it than that. These people are haunted. Every single one of them. And we don't know by what yet. So here's my question for listeners: What do you think the Fold actually does to people?
